coredata.dc:title: Walnut shell biomass waste derived excitation-dependent CQDs for toxic insecticide sensing and protein denaturation inhibition – An ecofriendly and sustainable approach
coredata.dc:creator: Devi, M. Saranya
Thangadurai, T. Daniel
Manjubaashini, N.
Nataraj, D.
coredata.dc:description: In the current predicament, converting any waste material into a valuable product is the need of the hour, and it demands a lot of confidence to work with. In an attempt to demonstrate this assertion, we converted one such waste material, Walnut Shell (WS), into Carbon Quantum Dot (CQD) for insecticide sensing and anti-inflammatory applications. WS is one such non-toxic waste material that has a carbon content and has been used for the preparation of CQDs hydrothermally. The amorphous nature of WS-derived CQDs (WS-CQDs) is revealed by the XRD results, which exhibit a distinct broad peak at 2θ from 21o to 29o. Microscopic images confirm the morphology of WS-CQDs as spherical, approximately 2 nm in size with average d-spacing fringes of 0.29 nm, and surface roughness of 12.53 nm. XPS, FTIR, and EDAX data were used to corroborate the chemical state, presence of functional groups, and elemental composition of WS-CQDs. The calculated ID/IG value (0.85) through Raman analysis divulges that the majority of the carbons in WS-CQDs are sp2 graphitic carbons with structural defects. Excitation-dependent properties of WS-CQDs were examined in different wavelengths (λmax 320 → 400 nm), and contour mapping analysis. Under optimized fluorescence experimental conditions, the linear range of imidacloprid was 0–80 μM with a detection limit of 7.58 × 10−6 M (r2 = 0.9904). The fluorescence decay profile of WS-CQDs suggests a tri-exponential function with an average lifetime of 4.10 ns. Additionally, experiments on the effects of time, competition, and pH on imidacloprid sensing have been performed. Biological properties like cytotoxicity, cell viability, and anti-inflammatory activity have also been investigated for WS-CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Walnut shell
CQDs
Imidacloprid
Anti-inflammatory
Denaturation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity MTT assay studies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD analysis of WS-CQDs, and (b) Raman spectrum of WS-CQDs.

(A) HRTEM images of WS-CQDs obtained at (a) 10 nm scale, (b) graphitic core lattice fringes from 2 nm scale, (c) histogram of particle size distribution, (d) SAED pattern of WS-CQDs; (B) AFM images of WS-CQDs (a) 2D and (b) 3D modes.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of WS-CQDs, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.

(a) PL emission spectra of WS-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (310 → 450 nm), (b) 3D fluorescence spectra of WS-CQDs (λex from 320 to 400 nm and corresponding emission), (c) Contour map analysis of WS-CQDs, and (d) Fluorescence decay profile of WS-CQDs in the absence and presence of imidacloprid (0.25, 0.50, and 1.0 μM) (λex 450 nm).

(a) Fluorescence emission changes of WS-CQDs with increased concentration of imidacloprid (0, 10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 μM) (λex 360 nm; slit width 1 nm), (b) Corresponding linear fit, and (c) Schematic representation of fluorescence ‘turn on’ binding mechanism between WS-CQDs and imidacloprid.

Examination of the cytotoxic effect of WS-CQDs (0, 5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μM) with MTT assay of L929 cell line. (a) (Control cells without adding WS-CQDs), (b) L929 cells + 5 μM WS-CQDs, (c) L929 cells + 25 μM WS-CQDs, (d) L929 cells + 50 μM WS-CQDs, (e) L929 cells + 75 μM WS-CQDs, and (f) L929 cells + 100 μM WS-CQDs.
Schematic representation of BSA protein denaturation inhibition by WS-CQDs through the formation of WS-CQDs-BSA complex.
Relative and average fluorescence lifetime of WS-CQDs upon addition of imidacloprid (0.25, 0.50, and 1.0 μM) (λex 450 nm).

head.ce:title: Walnut shell biomass waste derived excitation-dependent CQDs for toxic insecticide sensing and protein denaturation inhibition – An ecofriendly and sustainable approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, and Centre for Nanoscience and Technology, KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore 641407, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, and Centre for Nanoscience and Technology
KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, and Centre for Nanoscience and Technology, KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore 641407, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Chennai 600025, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Chennai 600025, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departments of Physics, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departments of Physics, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current predicament, converting any waste material into a valuable product is the need of the hour, and it demands a lot of confidence to work with. In an attempt to demonstrate this assertion, we converted one such waste material, Walnut Shell (WS), into Carbon Quantum Dot (CQD) for insecticide sensing and anti-inflammatory applications. WS is one such non-toxic waste material that has a carbon content and has been used for the preparation of CQDs hydrothermally. The amorphous nature of WS-derived CQDs (WS-CQDs) is revealed by the XRD results, which exhibit a distinct broad peak at 2θ from 21o to 29o. Microscopic images confirm the morphology of WS-CQDs as spherical, approximately 2 nm in size with average d-spacing fringes of 0.29 nm, and surface roughness of 12.53 nm. XPS, FTIR, and EDAX data were used to corroborate the chemical state, presence of functional groups, and elemental composition of WS-CQDs. The calculated ID/IG value (0.85) through Raman analysis divulges that the majority of the carbons in WS-CQDs are sp2 graphitic carbons with structural defects. Excitation-dependent properties of WS-CQDs were examined in different wavelengths (λmax 320 → 400 nm), and contour mapping analysis. Under optimized fluorescence experimental conditions, the linear range of imidacloprid was 0–80 μM with a detection limit of 7.58 × 10−6 M (r2 = 0.9904). The fluorescence decay profile of WS-CQDs suggests a tri-exponential function with an average lifetime of 4.10 ns. Additionally, experiments on the effects of time, competition, and pH on imidacloprid sensing have been performed. Biological properties like cytotoxicity, cell viability, and anti-inflammatory activity have also been investigated for WS-CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Food consumption is escalating quickly owing to the increasing global population, leading to a rise in agricultural wastes, which are known to be rich in lignocelluloses [1]. Biological resources are depleted and the environment is polluted when the majority of farm and agricultural wastes are burned, buried, or fed to animals. Husks, leaves, cotton, seaweed, coconut shells, and other biomass wastes can all be converted into carbon sources [2]. It's exciting to note that a variety of carbon allotropes, including graphene, graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon-based dots (carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbon-dots (CDs)), have been prepared by using these low-cost carbon sources [3,4]. Particularly, the carbon allotropes prepared from biomass wastes displayed superior physicochemical properties owing to the higher content of carbon (77.72 %) in them [5,6].

CQDs are sp2 carbon hybridized quasi-spherical carbogenic materials that are <10 nm in size and have a zero-dimensional (0D) structure [7]. They possess several unique and promising properties, including excellent chemical stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, low fabrication cost, and high quantum confinement due to chemical inertness [8]. CQDs were frequently made from molecular carbon precursors utilizing arc discharge, laser ablation [9], chemical oxidation [10], hydrothermal [11], thermal decomposition [12], and microwave [13] techniques to get high yields [9,14,15]. However, these methods have some drawbacks that limit the usage of CQDs in various applications [16,17]. Unsurprisingly, numerous chemical-free green synthesis techniques employing naturally available materials or sources for CQDs have already been reported [18]. For example, CQDs are prepared from fennel seeds and lemon juice [15], but there aren't many for biomass waste [19,20]. Although these synthesized CQDs have been utilized in applications such as bioimaging, sensors, and solar cells [21,22], and especially in the detection of various analytes [23,24], none for insecticide.

Insecticides are formulated to kill, hurt, repel, or mitigate one or more insect species which include ovicides and larvicides [25]. Some insecticides cause nervous system disruption, while others may harm their exoskeletons, repel them, or exert other forms of control [26,27]. Since insecticides are utilized in agriculture, medicine, industry, and by consumers, almost all insecticides tend to drastically change ecosystems; many are hazardous to people and/or animals; others concentrate as they move upwards in the food chain [28]. Imidacloprid, a new nicotinoid insecticide with a high level of efficiency, is a common agricultural insecticide used extensively on bananas, tomatoes, and other plants. While imidacloprid has been categorized as a moderately toxic and generally harmless insecticide, some research has indicated that its residues in agricultural environments and food are hazardous to human health and that its toxicity may be overestimated [29]. In addition to that, it was found that like other insecticides they harm non-target organisms. Imidacloprid and other members of the first generation are highly toxic to agriculture. Importantly, monitoring imidacloprid residues in agricultural products and producing locations is required [30]. Therefore, it is essential to develop an eco-friendly, sustainable method that uses practical, affordable materials for the efficient, rapid detection of imidacloprid.

In light of the details above, utilizing biomass waste from walnut shell (WS) as a non-acidic and harmless carbon source, herein we propose a hydrothermal method for the synthesis of WS-CQDs. This straightforward, environmentally benign, and sustainable approach yields spherical, amorphous WS-CQDs with an average size of 2 nm. A thorough analysis of the surface topography, structural defects, excitation-dependent characteristics, and impact-on lifetime was performed. The effect of pH, time, and competitiveness in imidacloprid sensing was also investigated. The present study's main objective is to explore the potential use of WS-CQDs in biological fields including cytotoxicity and anti-inflammatory responses as well as insecticide detection.

The synthesis of WS-CQDs was performed by hydrothermal method (Scheme S1). Initially, Walnuts were purchased from a local market and subsequently break down shells. The shells are washed with deionized (DI) water and dried in sunlight (24 h) and roasted at a 200 °C temperature. The initial brownish-colored WS turn into black and then meshed to obtain a fine black powder. 0.72 g of the fine WS black powder was dissolved in 36 mL of DI water and stirred for 30 min at 40 °C to get the homogenous solution. This homogeneous solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept in the oven for 3 h at 200 °C. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged and filtered by a 0.22 μm (PVDF) membrane. Finally, the yellow solution which contains WS-CQDs was collected and characterized for further studies.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was collected on Bruker D8 advance with the sources of Cu-Kα (1.56 Å) radiation. Surface morphology was analyzed on FESEM SIGMA HV-carl Zeiss with Bruker quanta 200-Z10 EDS' detector at Coimbatore Institute of Technology, Coimbatore, India. Raman spectra were captured using a spectrometer of (Alpha 300RA) at MGU, Kottayam, India. AFM images were recorded on the Alpha300RA model at SAIF, MGU, Kottayam, India. The contour map analysis was carried out using a fluorescence spectrometer (Fluorolog 3TCSPC, Horiba) with a lifetime detector at MGU, Kottayam, India. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a SHIMADZU at Avinashilingam University, Coimbatore, India. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyzed by XPS, VGESCA-LABMKIIX at SRM University, Chennai, India. The absorption spectra of UV analysis were carried on a Shimadzu UV-1900 spectrometer. Fluorescence analysis was carried out with a JASCO FP-6500 spectrofluorometer equipped with a Xenon discharge lamp at Ghandigram University, Dindigul, India. The IBH data station software V2.6 (Horiba - Deltaflax) was used to perform the time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements using the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. Cytotoxicity was analyzed at the South India Textile Research Association (SITRA), Coimbatore, India. Anti-inflammatory analysis of an ELISA plate analyzer (ROBONIK) at the University of Madras, Chennai, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity MTT assay studies
ce:section.ce:para:
Methyl Thiazolyl Tetrazolium (MTT) assay was used to determine the cell viability of WS-CQDs against L929 cells. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) supplemented in MEM medium was used to incubate L929 cells. MEM medium was used to cultivate the L929 cells at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 environment. Following a 24 h incubation period, the cells were treated at various concentrations of WS-CQDs solution (0, 0.5, 2.5, 5, 7.5, and 1 μM). The Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) with a pH of 7.2 was used to wash the cells completely. MTT (1 mg/mL) was then added to each cell well, and the cells were incubated for an additional 3 to 4 h. Once the incubation period was completed, the culture medium was withdrawn. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO; 100 %) was added to dissolve the crystallization of formazan. Inverted phase contrast microscopy was used to detect the optical absorbance of each solution at 570 nm to estimate the cell viability by using the following equation [31].
(1)
Cell viability =
O
D e
O
D c
× 100 %
where ODe is the absorbance of the experimental group, and ODc is the absorbance of the control group.

Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) (1 % aqueous solution) was combined with 0.05 mL of various concentrations of WS-CQDs (10, 30, and 50 μL), and a small quantity of 1.0 N HCl was used to bring the pH of the solution to 6.3. These samples were first heated at 37 °C for 20 min, followed by 30 min of heating at 60 °C in a water bath. After the sample solutions attained RT, the absorbance at 660 nm was determined by using a spectrophotometer. Diclofenac sodium was used as a conventional medication. Three duplicates of the experiment were run. The following equation was used to determine the percentage inhibition of protein denaturation [32].
(2)
% of inhibition =
Absorbance of control − Absorbance of test sample
Absorbance of control
× 100

Walnut, from the Juglandaceae family, is very hard, chemically inert, biodegradable, and non-toxic [33]. According to statistics, 3.8 million tons of walnuts were grown worldwide in 2017, and the shells are either burned or dumped into landfills [34]. The endocarp of a WS contains higher amounts of hydrophobic (lignin, 36.90 %) and hygroscopic (cellulose 17.74 %, and hemicellulose 36.06 % [35], thus, the CQDs prepared from the biomass waste of WS (Scheme S1) might have excellent optoelectronic properties which are suitable for sensitive and selective insecticide detection. Cheng et al. prepared WS-based CQDs using HNO3 and H2SO4 at 140 °C in an oil bath for 12 h [36]. However, in the current report, the WS-CQDs was synthesized by using only carbonized WS as a precursor at three different temperatures such as 160, 180, and 200 °C for 3 h by hydrothermal method. Among the three different temperatures employed, the uniform size CQDs (1–10 nm) was obtained when the synthesis was carried out at 200 °C. The HRTEM analysis confirmed the uniform size of CQDs. This green synthesis approach was carried out without adding any chemical additives which might be highly suitable for biological applications such as bioimaging, and anti-inflammatory studies.

The XRD diffraction analysis for WS-CQDs was carried out to identify their structural and crystalline nature. Noticeably, a distinct broad peak at 2θ from 21° to 29°, and two more broad shoulder peaks appeared at 2θ 42° and 55° are assigned to (002), (101), and (103) planes, respectively (Fig. 1a). These broad peaks indicate the poor crystalline nature of WS-CQDs. This amorphous nature of WS-CQDs may be caused by the presence of a large number of oxygen-containing groups and tightly packed carbon atoms [37], which may be attributed to the sp2 graphitic crystal phase of graphene nature in WS-CQDs. Using Bragg's law, the interlayer spacing of WS-CQDs was calculated and found to be 0.34 nm, which is almost equal to the interlayer spacing of the graphite (0.33 nm) [38]. The XRD diffraction data suggest that WS-CQDs are graphitic in nature.

To determine the morphology and surface topography of WS-CQDs, microscopic techniques such as FESEM, HRTEM, and AFM were employed. The FESEM images reveal that WS-CQDs are in a spherical shape (Fig. S1a), and the presence of carbon (45.47 %), nitrogen (1.20 %), and oxygen (53.33 %) are confirmed by EDS (Fig. S1b). The existence of elemental nitrogen (1.20 %) in WS-CQDs is due to the absorbed atmospheric nitrogen on the surface during the experimental and/or characterization. HRTEM images affirm that the average size of WS-CQDs is 2–3 nm (Fig. 2A(c)) with an average lattice spacing of 0.29 nm (Fig. 2A(b)), which is due to their interlayer spacing of graphitic nature [39]. Diffracted rings in the Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern suggests that WS-CQDs are not crystals but an amorphous structure of polycrystalline which is in harmony with XRD results (Fig. 2A(d)) [37]. Furthermore, the AFM images of WS-CQDs in 2D and 3D modes demonstrate the topographical surface roughness of 12.53 nm (Fig. 2B(a) and 2B(b)), and WS-CQDs were almost in spherical like structure [40].

The chemical composition and chemical bonds of WS-CQDs were further investigated by the XPS technique. The XPS survey scans of WS-CQDs show three peaks at 286, 400.9, and 532.6 eV, which are corresponds to C1s, N1s, and O1s, with atomic percentages of 76.5 %, 2.5 %, and 21.0 %, respectively (Fig. 3a). Two deconvoluted peaks were observed in the high-resolution spectrum of C1s. The peak at 284 eV is assigned to the sp2 hybrid carbon (CC) and 286 eV is attributed to the sp3 hybrid carbon (CC/CN) (Fig. 3b). The O1s spectrum shows two peaks at 532 and 533.0 eV which corresponds to CO and CO groups [41,42] respectively (Fig. 3d), revealing the formation of oxygen functional groups on the surface of WS-CQDs. The presence of low-content carbon-bonded nitrogen (CN) in WS-CQDs was indicated by a peak at 400.9 eV in N1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3c) [43]. This divulges that the nitrogen group is attached to the WS-CQDs surface. From XPS data, it is evident that the hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups were present on the surface of WS-CQDs, which is advantageous for surface modification and functionalization as well as for the materials' solubility in water [19].

In the Raman spectra of WS-CQDs, the disorder (D) band observed at 1340 cm−1 is caused by the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon [44]. However, the graphite (G) band perceived at 1572 cm−1 is caused by the graphite's E2g mode and the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice (Fig. 1b) [45]. The intensity of the G band is greater than that of the D band (ID/IG = 0.85) indicating that the WS-CQDs are primarily composed of sp2 graphitic carbons with defects [46]. Notably, there is a small peak that appears at 1437 cm−1 which represents the existence of CH vibration in the Raman spectrum [47].

The presence of functional groups in WS-CQDs was confirmed by the FTIR spectrum. The hydroxyl functional group (υOH) peak was observed at 3857 and 3263 cm−1 (Fig. S2). A less intense peak witnessed at 2306 cm−1 is attributed to the sp3 CH bond vibration. A sharp peak that appeared at 1635 cm−1 is corresponding to the carbonyl group (υCO) attached to the benzene ring. The other peak that appeared at 1527 cm−1 is due to υN-O stretching bond, and 671 cm−1 is represented as a benzene derivative of WS-CQDs [48]. All of these stretching and vibration bands divulge the presence of various functional groups such as hydroxyl, and carboxyl attached to the surface of WS-CQDs which is in accord with the XPS result [49].

UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectral analyses were carried out to determine the optical characteristics of WS-CQDs. The UV–Visible spectra of WS-CQDs exhibit a sharp absorption peak at 278 nm transition (Fig. S3), which is probably associated with the aromatic CC bond (π → π* transition) [50]. To determine the excitation wavelength-dependent property, the WS-CQDs solution was excited at different wavelengths (from 310 to 450 nm) (Fig. 4a). The WS-CQDs display strongly increased fluorescence emission intensity upon exciting from 310 to 360 nm wavelength [51]. However, when the excitation wavelength was increased further (from 370 to 450 nm), the emission intensity gradually decreased. Maximum emission intensity was attained at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Noteworthy to mention that the fluorescence emission peaks show a redshift as the excitation wavelength increases indicating the existence of photon reabsorption [52]. This outcome demonstrates that the photoluminescence property of WS-CQDs is excitation-dependent. When their excitation wavelength varies, blue fluorescent WS-CQDs show a broad photoluminescence peak owing to the quantum-confinement effect and edge defects [19].

The three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence emission spectra of the WS-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (from 320 to 400 nm in 5 nm intervals) are shown in Fig. 4b. Excitation and emission contour maps of WS-CQDs show their multicolor photoluminescence characteristics (Fig. 4c). Additionally, the surface chemistry of the particles has a sizable impact on the fluorescence characteristics of WS-CQDs. From the contour image, it is confirmed that WS-CQDs exhibit excitation light-dependent emission properties. In general, there are two typical emission models responsible for this property: (i) bandgap transitions in conjugated π-domains, and (ii) surface defects in WS-CQDs. According to the former mechanism, the quantum size impact is attributed to the redshift in the luminescence of CQDs [53]. In the latter mechanism, the surface state is the main factor affecting the fluorescence change. In this study, the excitation wavelength dependent on WS-CQDs may be caused by surface state defects [54] which is in good harmony with Raman analysis. An elongation is observed at the longer wavelength side of the contour pattern which is due to the tail-like structure observed at the red edge of the excitation-dependent emission spectra which is in turn due to the enhanced shift in PL maximum at longer wavelength excitations [55]. Even the contour graph showed that the blue region defines the range of less particle size whereas the red region defines the range of highest particle size. The average FWHM measured from the fluorescence emission was 91 nm, confirming the produced WS-CQDs' narrow size dispersion, and the photoluminescence properties of their WS-CQDs are highly correlated with the WS-CQD concentration [51].

To apply WS-CQDs as insecticide sensing material, the sensitivity of WS-CQDs towards imidacloprid was investigated by fluorescence titration studies. The fluorescence emission intensity of WS-CQDs was enhanced upon the addition of different concentrations of imidacloprid (0–80 μM) (Fig. 5a). During the addition of imidacloprid to the WS-CQDs solution, a ground state complex was formed due to the interaction between carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of WS-CQDs and imidacloprid [56,57]. In addition, the existing active NH2 functional group on the surface of WS-CQDs promotes a potent interaction with the imidacloprid molecule. When exposed to imidacloprid, WS-CQDs' emission wavelength shifted to a low wavelength (blue-shift; 441–436 nm; 5 nm), which may be associated with their ability to donate protons (Fig. 5c) [58]. Furthermore, the presence of imidacloprid in the WS-CQDs solution might reduce the self-agglomeration of WS-CQDs due to the strong binding between WS-CQDs and imidacloprid. In general, molecules can exhibit excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) if their structures incorporate an intramolecular hydrogen bonding through the interaction between a hydrogen bond donor (–OH and/or NH2) and a hydrogen bond acceptor (N and/or CO) [59]. The ESIPT is considered a process where the proton is exchanged through intramolecular hydrogen bonding between the proton donating and accepting groups in the molecule. In the current sensing studies, the analyte molecule, imidacloprid, has highly reactive NO2 and NH groups which might be coordinated with the NH2, COOH, and OH groups on the surface of the WS-CQDs, breaking the self-complexation between two or more CQDs and causing them to become even more detached. Thus, the WS-CQDs show a more intense fluorescence nature in the non-detachable state, which is further reflected as a strong binding affinity of WS-CQDs. Further, a similar type of mechanism was reported by P. Mandal et al. [60]. There are also fluorescence turn on systems in the literature [61], where the proton donating and accepting sites are not adjacent and hence the proton is transferred over a long distance. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by F/F0 versus [Q] which is almost linear within the concentration range of 0–80 μM (where F0 and F are the fluorescence emission intensities of the WS-CQDs in the absence and presence of the insecticide, respectively) (Fig. 5b). The calculated binding constant (Ka = 1.081 × 10−3 M−1), and LoD (7.58 μM) from the fluorescence titration experiment (Fig. S4) led us to conclude that WS-CQDs could be effective ‘turn on’ fluorescent sensors showing high sensitivity towards imidacloprid.

A time-correlated single-photon counting experiment was carried out to examine the fluorescence lifetime profile of WS-CQDs in the presence and absence of imidacloprid (Fig. 4d). Initially, in the absence of imidacloprid, the fluorescence lifetime of WS-CQDs is best fitted with a tri-exponential function with an average lifetime of 4.10 ns (Table 1 ). The obtained slow decay is due to the effective relaxation of carriers from the WS-CQDs core onto the surface states [62]. The obtained nanosecond (ns) range fluorescence lifetime indicates that WS-CQDs have a singlet state nature [63]. This tri-exponential function fitting (Eq. (3)) explicates that fluorescence decay occurs through three relaxation pathways.
(3)
F
t
=
A 1
exp .
− t /
τ 1
+
A 2
exp .
− t /
τ 2
+
A 3
exp .
− t /
τ 3
where “τ” is the fluorescence lifetime and “A” represents the amplitude of the corresponding lifetime. Upon the addition of a low concentration of imidacloprid (0.25 eq.), the decay profile curve of WS-CQDs showed an increase in average lifetime value (4.10 → 4.56 ns) without changing its functionality. On the other hand, increasing the concentration of imidacloprid (0.50 and 1.0 eq.) decreases the fluorescence lifetime of WS-CQDs (3.88 and 3.18 ns, respectively), which may be due to the resulting ground-state complex frequency responses [64]. This ground-sate complex formation phenomenon is well matched with fluorescence titration study results (Fig. 5a) and binding mechanism (Fig. 5c). Overall, the tri-exponential decay observed in WS-CQDs designates the presence of multiple radiative decay pathways in the corresponding QD system [65].

Fluorescence emission intensity changes of WS-CQDs upon binding with imidacloprid were examined in different pH. To attain acidic and basic nature, the pH of WS-CQDs+imidacloprid solution ranging from 1 to 7 and 7 to 13 was prepared by the addition of HNO3 and KOH, respectively. The fluorescence emission intensity was linearly increased at 436 nm in the acidic condition (Fig. S5a). In contrast, the emission intensity was decreased at 441 nm in basic conditions (Fig. S5b). Interestingly, a transparent to yellowish-brown color change was also visualized when the pH value of the WS-CQDs+imidacloprid solution increased from 1 to 13. The variation of electronic transitions such as π → π* and n → π* in graphite nanodomains by filling or depleting their valence bands might be the reason for this color change in different pH [66]. Thus, we infer that different functional groups attached to the surface of WS-CQDs were responsible for the changes in the fluorescence intensity and color upon pH variation.

WS-CQDs undergo an immediate fluorescence enhancement at 436 nm upon the addition of imidacloprid (100 μM) and require <1 min to attain >98 % of maximum fluorescence emission intensity. After one minute, no changes were observed in the initially attained fluorescence emission intensity even though the reaction time increased up to 10 min. This suggests that sensing of imidacloprid by WS-CQDs is stable and extremely fast (Fig. S6). To confirm the strong binding of WS-CQD and imidacloprid, interference studies were performed using other toxic insecticides such as acetamiprid (AC), fipronil (FI), and piranha (PI) in the presence of imidacloprid. The addition of a 100-fold higher concentration (100 μM) of the above-mentioned three toxic insecticide solutions to WS-CQDs+imidacloprid solution, quenches the initial fluorescence intensity of WS-CQDs which is negligible and might be due to the dilution effect (Fig. S7). This phenomenon confirms that the interfering insecticides do not compete with imidacloprid, either not binding or very weak binding with WS-CQDs, and WS-CQD binds with imidacloprid more strongly.

CQDs are appropriate materials for biological applications like drug delivery and cell bioimaging due to their small particle size and unique biological characteristics [67]. To prove this notion, it is essential to examine the interaction between a cell and WS-CQDs to commence. L929 cells were used to conduct an MTT assay experiment to analyze the cell viability of WS-CQDs. The cell viability was analyzed when the cells were cultured in WS-CQDs solution at various concentrations (5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μM) for 24 h (Fig. 6 ). After 24 h of incubation, the cytotoxicity values were found to be 13, 33, 57, 67, and 87 %, respectively (Fig. S9a). As per ISO/IEC 17025, the standard chart was used to determine the cytotoxic reactivity of WS-CQDs against L929 cells (Fig. S8b). Fig. S9a indicates the slight to moderate cytotoxicity (33 %) in the high concentration (100 μM) after a 24 h incubation period for L929 cells. The computed IC50 value was calculated to be 1.58 μM (Fig. S8a), which is determined by the time of contact assay.

It has been established that one of the inflammations is protein denaturation which in turn leads to inflammation in a small number of rheumatic illnesses [68]. Anti-inflammatory medicines can be used to prevent protein denaturation at high temperatures [69]. However, commonly used anti-inflammatory medicine has side effects [70], for example, gastrointestinal discomfort. Based on this hypothesis, we performed the denaturation of BSA with WS-CQDs. The effective interaction between WS-CQDs and BSA is systematically represented in Fig. 7 . The subdomains IIA (Sudlows site I: warfarin-binding site) and IIIA (Sudlows site II: indole/benzodiazepine site) in BSA interact with WS-CQDs through two major binding sites (Fig. 7) [68,71]. Site II seems to be smaller or narrower than Site I, which appears to be more expansive. Considerably, Site II is less flexible than site I because stereoselectivity frequently has a major impact on binding with WS-CQDs [71]. Particularly, Site I offer binding affinity predominantly via hydrophobic interactions with WS-CQDs, in contrast to site II, which combines hydrophobic, hydrogen-binding, and electrostatic interactions. Fig. S9b divulges that BSA protein denaturation was significantly inhibited by the anti-inflammatory activity of WS-CQD. i.e. increasing concentrations of WS-CQD (10, 30, and 50 μL) does not show any level of inhibition changes. Therefore, it is clear that WS-CQD showed a potential inhibition of BSA protein denaturation even at lower concentrations of WS-CQDs (10 μL).

In summary, WS-CQDs were successfully prepared by hydrothermal method using WS as a precursor without adding any chemicals. The XRD and TEM analysis indicated that the synthesized WS-CQDs possess an amorphous graphitic carbon-like structure with a narrow size distribution of 2–3 nm. The Raman spectroscopy confirmed the presence of defects and the amorphous nature of WS-CQDs due to the presence of various functional groups on the surface. XPS characterization clearly showed that WS-CQDs predominantly consist of carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups. The formation of WS-CQDs-imidacloprid complex was confirmed by fluorescence sensing of imidacloprid by WS-CQDs with LOD of 7.58 μM and binding constant Ka = 1.081 × 10−3 M−1. Further evidence for the formation of a ground-state complex between WS-CQDs and imidacloprid is provided by the obtained tri-exponential fluorescence lifetime for WS-CQDs in the nanosecond range (4.10 ns) and reduced lifetime (3.18 ns) in the absence and presence of imidacloprid, respectively. The contour map analysis supports the excitation wavelength dependency of WS-CQDs through the existence of various size groups of particles. In the supporting information, a comparison table for imidacloprid detection has been provided (Table S1). The WS-CQDs exhibit a slight to moderate cytotoxicity (67 %) with 87 % cell viability and an IC50 value of 1.58 μM against L929 cells. The anti-inflammatory studies show BSA protein denaturation is inhibited to not <95 %. These attained interesting results recommend that WS-biomass-derived CQDs can be a good potential candidate for several biological applications.

M. Saranya Devi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Experiment, Data curation, formal analysis, and writing-original draft. T. Daniel Thangadurai: writing-review & Editing, and supervision, validation, Investigation, Data curation. N. Manjubhaashini: XPS analysis, data validation & anti-inflammatory studies. D. Nataraj: Raman data validation, writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors MSD and TDT respectfully thank the KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology for research facilities, institutional research fellowship, and a seed grant. NM acknowledges the DBT-Research Associateship program, and DN expresses appreciation for the UGC-CPEPA program at Bharathiar University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110021.
